nodes.task:
Fix Bug 1: `reconcileAgents()` in src/controller/server.ts drops `workflowStep` / `workflowName`, so after `flt controller stop && flt controller start` any agent running in a workflow gets `Error: No running workflow found for agent "<name>"` when it calls `flt workflow pass`.

TDD discipline: write the failing test FIRST, watch it fail, then implement the fix, then watch it pass.

## Files to modify
- src/controller/server.ts — `reconcileAgents()` (currently spans roughly lines 162–249) reads tmux env vars and calls `setAgent` with `cli, model, tmuxSession, parentName, dir, worktreePath, worktreeBranch, spawnedAt` but does NOT set `workflowStep` or `workflowName`. Fix: derive both from the tmux env that flt always sets when spawning workflow agents:
  - `workflowName`: `path.basename(env.FLT_RUN_DIR)` (e.g. FLT_RUN_DIR=/Users/x/.flt/runs/my-run → `my-run`).
  - `workflowStep`: read `<env.FLT_RUN_DIR>/run.json`, JSON.parse it, take `currentStep`. Tolerate missing file / parse errors — if either env var is missing or the file is unreadable, do NOT set those fields (an agent spawned outside a workflow has no FLT_RUN_DIR). Use `fs.readFileSync` synchronously since reconcileAgents is already a sync function.
  - Pass the derived `workflowStep`, `workflowName` into the `setAgent(name, { … })` call near the bottom of the function. The `AgentState` type already accepts these fields (engine.ts and other call sites set them); confirm with a quick grep of the type definition in src/state.ts before assuming.
- src/controller/server.ts — to make the function unit-testable, refactor minimally: extract the per-session derivation into a small exported pure function `deriveWorkflowFromEnv(env: Record<string, string>, readFileFn?: (p: string) => string): { workflowName?: string; workflowStep?: string }`. Default `readFileFn` to `(p) => fs.readFileSync(p, 'utf-8')` so tests can inject a fake. Have `reconcileAgents` call this helper for each session. Keep the helper at module scope and `export` it. Do NOT export `reconcileAgents` itself.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/controller-reconcile.test.ts — NEW file. Use bun:test (`import { test, expect, describe } from 'bun:test'`). Cover the pure helper directly — no real tmux, no real fs:
  1. Given `FLT_RUN_DIR=/tmp/runs/abc-123` and a fake readFileFn that returns `JSON.stringify({ currentStep: 'spec' })`, `deriveWorkflowFromEnv` returns `{ workflowName: 'abc-123', workflowStep: 'spec' }`.
  2. Given no `FLT_RUN_DIR`, returns `{}` (no fields).
  3. Given `FLT_RUN_DIR` set but readFileFn throws (file missing) — returns `{ workflowName: 'abc-123' }` only (workflowStep absent). Use a readFileFn that throws.
  4. Given run.json content with no `currentStep` field — workflowStep absent, workflowName still derived from basename.
  5. Given `FLT_RUN_DIR` with a trailing slash (`/tmp/runs/abc-123/`) — workflowName must still equal `abc-123` (verify your implementation uses `path.basename` correctly or strips trailing slash before basename).

Run the test BEFORE editing src/controller/server.ts to confirm it fails (compile error or assertion failure). Record the failure in your handoff.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/controller-reconcile.test.ts` — green after fix.
- `bun test` — full suite green (no new failures introduced). Run from the worktree root.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors compared to the pre-change baseline. Capture both numbers in your handoff.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- No drive-by refactors. Only the helper extraction described is allowed.
- Do NOT change `setAgent`'s signature or the AgentState type if `workflowStep` / `workflowName` are already optional fields on it. If they are NOT, add them as optional and document why in your handoff.
- Keep error handling tight — wrap the readFileSync + JSON.parse in a single try/catch and silently skip on any failure (the controller must keep starting up even if one agent's run.json is corrupt).

Write a coder.md handoff summarising the diff at file granularity, the pre-change failing test output, the post-change green output, and the tsc baseline numbers.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Fix Bug 2: `flt kill <workflow-agent>` flips the workflow's `run.json` `status` to `cancelled` and stops advancement. Killing a single node should mark THAT node as failed (engine then handles via its normal failure path), not cancel the whole workflow.

TDD discipline: write the failing test FIRST, watch it fail, then implement the fix, then watch it pass.

## Files to modify
- src/commands/kill.ts — `killDirect()` currently contains (around line 109–117 — confirm exact line range with grep):
  ```
  if (!args.fromWorkflow) {
    try {
      const { getWorkflowForAgent, cancelWorkflow } = require('../workflow/engine') as typeof import('../workflow/engine')
      const workflowId = getWorkflowForAgent(name)
      if (workflowId) {
        cancelWorkflow(workflowId).catch(() => {})
      }
    } catch {}
  }
  ```
  Replace `cancelWorkflow(workflowId)` with a call to `handleStepFailure(workflowId)` (also exported from src/workflow/engine.ts — confirm the export with grep). The dynamic require still needs to import `handleStepFailure` instead of `cancelWorkflow`. Keep the `if (!args.fromWorkflow)` guard — engine-initiated kills must still bypass.
  Rationale: handleStepFailure marks the current step as failed and lets the engine's normal advance loop run on_fail / retry / abort logic — which is exactly what should happen when the controller's cleanupDeadAgent observes a dead agent. cancelWorkflow flips status to `cancelled` and stops the run; that path is reserved for explicit `flt workflow cancel`.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/kill-workflow-cascade.test.ts — NEW file. Pure unit, NOT integration. Use bun:test. Approach:
  1. The cleanest path is to extract the engine-notification block into a small exported helper inside src/commands/kill.ts: `export async function notifyEngineOfKill(name: string, fromWorkflow: boolean | undefined, engine?: { getWorkflowForAgent: (n: string) => string | null; handleStepFailure: (id: string) => Promise<void> }): Promise<void>`. The helper defaults `engine` to a lazy `require('../workflow/engine')`. killDirect calls this helper. Tests call it with an injected fake engine.
  2. Test cases against the helper:
     - When `fromWorkflow` is unset and the fake's `getWorkflowForAgent` returns `'run-1'`, the fake's `handleStepFailure` is called once with `'run-1'`.
     - The fake exposes a `cancelWorkflow` spy (even though kill.ts no longer imports it); assert the spy is NEVER invoked from this code path.
     - When `fromWorkflow` is `true`, neither spy is called.
     - When `getWorkflowForAgent` returns `null`, neither spy is called.
  3. If extracting a helper feels too invasive, alternative: use bun's `mock.module` (search existing tests for prior art — e.g. tests/unit/spawn.test.ts) to mock '../../src/workflow/engine' and assert via the mocked exports. Pick whichever path keeps the test cleanest and document the choice in coder.md.

Run the chosen test BEFORE editing src/commands/kill.ts to confirm it fails (the helper doesn't exist yet, OR the assertion that handleStepFailure is called fails because the code currently calls cancelWorkflow). Record.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/kill-workflow-cascade.test.ts` — green after fix.
- `bun test` — full suite green (no new failures). Run from worktree root.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors vs baseline. Capture both numbers.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- No drive-by refactors beyond the helper extraction described above.
- Do NOT remove or change the `if (!args.fromWorkflow)` guard.
- Do NOT touch src/workflow/engine.ts (handleStepFailure is already exported and used by the controller's cleanupDeadAgent path).
- Do NOT also create tests/integration/kill-workflow-agent.test.ts — the unit test is sufficient.

Write a coder.md handoff summarising the diff, pre/post test output, and tsc numbers.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Fix Bug 3: dynamic-feature workflow opens TWO PRs — one from `flt/<slug>-plan` (just plan.json, the bug) and one from `flt/<slug>-execute-integration` (the real work, correct). Only steps with `auto_pr_step: true` should push + open a PR; all other steps should commit-only.

TDD discipline: write the failing test FIRST, watch it fail, then implement the fix, then watch it pass.

## Diagnosis to run BEFORE editing
Read src/workflow/engine.ts lines ~560–640 (the `applyAutoCommit` and `shouldCreatePr` functions) and find every call site of `applyAutoCommit` (grep `applyAutoCommit` — there are calls around lines 372, 379, 1368). Determine WHY a PR is being opened on the plan step despite only the `pr` step having `auto_pr_step: true` in templates/workflows/dynamic-feature.yaml (line 148).

Likely root causes (verify which one applies — do not blindly patch):
1. `shouldCreatePr` returns true correctly (only flagged step), but a call site passes `commitOnly=false` for the plan step and the function still pushes the branch (the push step is BEFORE the shouldCreatePr early-return inside the body). Re-read applyAutoCommit carefully: the `git push -u origin <branch>` block is inside `if (agent.worktreeBranch && !run.vars._pr)`. The `commitOnly || !shouldCreatePr` early-return GUARDS that block. So if shouldCreatePr returns false, push is skipped. Confirm.
2. The `commitOnly` parameter is mishandled — e.g. one call site passes `false` for the plan step and shouldCreatePr is also returning true for the plan step (perhaps because dynamic-feature.yaml has multiple `auto_pr_step: true` steps in earlier revisions, or because `def.auto_pr` is unset and the `flagged.length > 0` logic is incorrect).
3. `gh pr create` is being invoked from a separate path entirely (e.g. an architect-step hook or a different workflow type). Search for any `gh pr create` outside applyAutoCommit.
4. `shellEscapeArg` or template resolution causes `applyAutoCommit` to fire on a step whose ID is mis-resolved.

Grep `gh pr create` repo-wide and `auto_pr_step` repo-wide. Read templates/workflows/dynamic-feature.yaml in full to confirm only one step has auto_pr_step: true. Then write the failing test against the actual root cause you find.

## Files likely to modify
- src/workflow/engine.ts — `applyAutoCommit` and/or `shouldCreatePr`. The fix MUST guarantee: when no step in a def has `auto_pr_step: true`, every step PRs (current default-true behaviour). When at least one step has it, ONLY those steps push + PR; all others commit-only. The current `shouldCreatePr` logic looks correct on paper — if the bug is elsewhere (e.g. an extra `gh pr create` call in another function), patch that instead.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/workflow-auto-pr.test.ts ALREADY EXISTS and tests `shouldCreatePr` (read it). Extend it (or add a sibling test file `tests/unit/workflow-auto-commit.test.ts` if cleaner) with a test that exercises `applyAutoCommit` end-to-end with a mocked execSync. Approach:
  1. Mock `child_process.execSync` (use bun's mock — see tests/unit/spawn.test.ts for how this repo mocks child_process). Capture every command invoked.
  2. Create a fake agent with `worktreePath: '/tmp/wt'`, `worktreeBranch: 'flt/repro-plan'`, no `instructionProjection`.
  3. Create a fake run with `vars: { _input: { task: 'demo' } }`, no `_pr` set.
  4. Create a fake def with two steps: `{ id: 'plan' }` (no auto_pr_step) and `{ id: 'pr', auto_pr_step: true }`. `def.auto_pr` unset.
  5. Call `applyAutoCommit(agent, run, def, 'plan')` and assert: NO captured command starts with `gh pr create`, NO command is `git push -u origin …`. A `git add -A && git commit …` IS expected.
  6. Call `applyAutoCommit(agent, run, def, 'pr')` and assert: a `gh pr create` command IS captured and a `git push -u origin flt/repro-plan` is captured.
  7. Both assertions in (5) must FAIL on `main` if the bug exists. If the test is green on main pre-fix, you have not reproduced the bug — re-investigate root cause until the test reproduces it.

`applyAutoCommit` is currently NOT exported. Export it (add `export` keyword to the `function` declaration) so the test can call it directly. That is the only API surface change allowed.

Run the test BEFORE editing the implementation to confirm it fails. Record the failure output in your handoff. If the test passes pre-fix you have NOT reproduced the bug — fix your test or your understanding before patching.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/workflow-auto-pr.test.ts` (and your new file if added) — green after fix.
- `bun test` — full suite green. No new failures.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors vs baseline. Capture both numbers.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- Do NOT modify any yaml workflow templates.
- Do NOT touch src/workflow/engine.ts outside the applyAutoCommit / shouldCreatePr region (or whichever single function the actual root cause lives in).
- Do NOT change call sites of applyAutoCommit unless the call site itself is the bug; prefer fixing the function body.

Write a coder.md handoff explaining the actual root cause you found, the diff, pre/post test output, and tsc numbers.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Fix Bug 4: `flt workflow run daily-mutator --task "test"` fails after ~150ms with `currentStep=mutate`, empty results/, empty handoffs/, no children spawned. The parallel step handler in src/workflow/engine.ts crashes silently before children spawn.

Depends on bug-3-auto-pr because both touch src/workflow/engine.ts; bug-3 lands first to avoid merge conflicts. Rebase on the bug-3 commit before starting.

TDD discipline: write a failing test FIRST that drives the diagnosis, then fix, then watch it pass.

## Diagnosis to run BEFORE editing
- Read `executeParallelStep` in src/workflow/engine.ts (starts ~line 849, runs through ~line 920). Trace its early-fail surface:
  - `parallelStep.step.preset` undefined when both `presets` and `step.preset` unset → throws.
  - `presets` array provided but `parallelStep.n` mismatched → `permuteTreatmentMap` may throw or produce a partial map.
  - `getPreset(presetName)` returns undefined → throws.
  - `resolveTemplate(parallelStep.step.task ?? '', run)` template-resolve failure (e.g. unresolved `{steps.foo.worktree}` reference) → throws.
  - `await spawn(...)` rejects for the first child (worktree-create failure, branch already exists) → no remaining children attempt; the throw propagates and the step fails fast.
  - Any exception is caught somewhere upstream and swallowed into `stepFailReason: undefined`, masking the real cause.
- Read templates/workflows/daily-mutator.yaml's `mutate` step. The bootstrap notes the existing plan.json that lives in this worktree's root references this — but the actual yaml is the source of truth. Confirm `mutate`'s shape (`type: parallel`, `n: 6`, `presets: [...]`, `step: {...}`).
- Reproduce locally if you can: `bun src/cli.ts workflow run daily-mutator --task test --no-spawn` — but a real run is fastest. If the run dir is already populated under ~/.flt/runs/daily-mutator/, inspect run.json and any error in events.log for clues.
- Identify the actual exception (likely candidates listed above). Patch surface:
  1. Wrap the body of `executeParallelStep` in a try/catch. On catch, set `run.stepFailReason = (err as Error).message` and `appendEvent({ type: 'workflow', detail: 'parallel step crashed: ' + msg })`, then rethrow so the engine's existing failure path runs. This is the diagnostic surface — without it the silent failure persists.
  2. Fix the actual root cause once identified. Likely: a missing preset in templates/presets/ (e.g. `cc-mutator` not registered), or a template variable in the child task that fails to resolve, or `presets` array length not equal to `n`.
  3. If the root cause is a missing preset registration, register it in src/presets.ts (or templates/presets/ if that's where preset configs live — confirm by grepping `cc-mutator` and `cc-opus`).

## Files likely to modify
- src/workflow/engine.ts — `executeParallelStep` (around lines 849–920). Add the try/catch error surface AND the actual fix for the underlying crash.
- Possibly src/presets.ts or templates/presets/cc-mutator.* if a preset is missing.
- Possibly templates/workflows/daily-mutator.yaml ONLY if the yaml itself has an unresolvable template variable; minimise yaml changes — prefer fixing engine logic.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/workflow-parallel.test.ts ALREADY EXISTS — read it first to understand existing test patterns. Add new tests there (or in a sibling tests/unit/workflow-parallel-crash.test.ts if the existing file is overcrowded). Cover:
  1. Crash surfacing: when `executeParallelStep` is called with a parallel step whose `presets` references an unknown preset name, the function throws an Error whose message names the preset. (This already happens via `getPreset` throw — but assert it does NOT get swallowed silently. The before-fix failure mode is silent — i.e. the run's stepFailReason is empty. The test should assert that after a crash, run.stepFailReason contains a non-empty diagnostic message.)
  2. Crash surfacing for any other forced-fail path: e.g. given `n: 2` but `presets: ['x']` (length mismatch), the function throws a clear error message naming the mismatch.
  3. Happy path regression: given a valid 2-child config with a registered preset, the function calls the spawn fn exactly twice with distinct `agentName` values and label `a`, `b`. Mock `getSpawnFn` to return a jest-style spy. (Many of the existing tests in workflow-parallel.test.ts already mock spawn — reuse that pattern.)
  4. If the actual root cause you found is something specific (e.g. unresolved template in child task), add a targeted regression test for that exact failure mode.

Run the new tests BEFORE patching to confirm at least one fails (the silent-failure surfacing test should fail because the function currently does NOT wrap its body in try/catch — depending on the existing code shape).

## Live integration check (manual, NOT automated)
After the fix lands, run `bun src/cli.ts workflow run daily-mutator --task test` once locally and confirm `currentStep` advances past `mutate` and at least one child agent spawns (or, if cc-mutator preset is missing, a clear stepFailReason in run.json names what's missing). Capture the run.json snippet in your coder.md.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/workflow-parallel.test.ts` (and any new file) — green.
- `bun test` — full suite green. No new failures.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors vs baseline.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- No drive-by refactors. Touch only the parallel-step handler region of engine.ts plus the actual root-cause fix.
- Do NOT change the existing yaml workflows beyond what's strictly required to fix the crash.
- Do NOT add new dependencies.

Write a coder.md handoff explaining: the actual root cause you diagnosed, the diff per file, the live `bun src/cli.ts workflow run daily-mutator` result snippet, pre/post test output, and tsc numbers.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Fix Bug 5: in `flt tui` on the workflows page (`w` key) and gates page (`g` key), the selected row's background highlight has GAPS at every vertical column separator and its surrounding padding. The selected row should appear as one continuous bar.

TDD discipline: write the failing test FIRST, watch it fail, then fix, then watch it pass.

## Diagnosis to run BEFORE editing
- Read src/tui/modal-workflows.ts `putSeparatedRow` (around lines 49–75). Note the comment at ~line 62: `// Separator never inherits ATTR_INVERSE — keeps the line glyph crisp on …`. Then the next line: `const sepAttrs = attrs & ~ATTR_INVERSE`. That's the bug. By stripping ATTR_INVERSE on the separator's attrs, when a row is selected (selected rows use ATTR_INVERSE so the terminal swaps fg↔bg → the row's fg color becomes the bg highlight), the separator and its surrounding padding revert to the table's default bg → visible gaps.
- Confirm the same `putSeparatedRow` exists (copy-pasted) in src/tui/modal-gates.ts (around line 252) and src/tui/metrics-modal.ts (around line 84). The first two definitely render selectable rows. Inspect each to verify behaviour. metrics-modal.ts likely does not render selection — confirm before editing.
- The fix: do NOT strip ATTR_INVERSE from sepAttrs. Pass the full `attrs` through to the separator's `screen.put` call so the separator cell gets the same inverse swap. The line glyph `│` will still render in the separator's fg, just on the inverse-swapped bg — that's the correct visual.
- Edge case: the table HEADER row also calls putSeparatedRow but is never selected (no ATTR_INVERSE in its attrs). So inheriting attrs as-is is safe. The horizontal-rule renderer (`putHorizontalRule`) is separate and not in scope.

## Files to modify
- src/tui/modal-workflows.ts — remove `const sepAttrs = attrs & ~ATTR_INVERSE` and pass `attrs` to the separator's `screen.put`. Update the comment to explain WHY (inverse must propagate so selected-row bg is continuous).
- src/tui/modal-gates.ts — same change at line ~252's putSeparatedRow.
- src/tui/metrics-modal.ts — apply the same change ONLY if its putSeparatedRow is also called with ATTR_INVERSE on selected rows. If metrics-modal never renders a selected row, leave it alone to keep the diff minimal. Document your decision in coder.md.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/tui-row-bg.test.ts — NEW file. Use bun:test. Approach:
  1. Import `putSeparatedRow` from src/tui/modal-workflows.ts (and src/tui/modal-gates.ts). It is NOT currently exported — add `export` keyword to the function declaration in both files. That's the only API change allowed.
  2. Build a fake Screen with a `put(row, col, text, fg, bg, attrs)` spy capturing every call. The Screen interface lives in src/tui/screen.ts — read it for the exact shape, but for the test you only need the `put` method, so a `{ put: ... }` minimal stub typed as `Pick<Screen, 'put'>` (or, if too narrow, a full interface implementation with no-op stubs for the other methods) is fine. No `as any`.
  3. Call `putSeparatedRow(screen, 0, 0, [4, 4], ['aaaa', 'bbbb'], '#fff', '#888', '', ATTR_INVERSE)` (header-style call but with ATTR_INVERSE forced).
  4. Among the recorded `put` calls, locate the one for the separator (` │ `). Assert its `attrs` argument has `ATTR_INVERSE` set (i.e. `(attrs & ATTR_INVERSE) !== 0`).
  5. Repeat for src/tui/modal-gates.ts's putSeparatedRow.
  6. The test must FAIL on `main` (current code strips ATTR_INVERSE from sepAttrs) and PASS after the fix.

Run the test BEFORE editing implementations. Confirm the failure and record it.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/tui-row-bg.test.ts` — green after fix.
- `bun test tests/unit/tui` — full TUI test dir green.
- `bun test` — full suite green. No new failures.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors vs baseline.
- Manual visual: skip — no live TUI test infra in this run. Note in handoff that visual confirmation is deferred to user.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- Do NOT consolidate the three putSeparatedRow copies into a shared helper. The duplication is pre-existing; refactoring it is out of scope.
- Do NOT add new exports beyond the `export` keywords needed for testability.
- Do NOT touch the horizontal-rule renderer or any other modal's row rendering.

Write a coder.md handoff with the diff per file, pre/post test output, and tsc numbers.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Fix Bug 6: in `flt tui` on the metrics page (`t` key), the horizontal cost/token bars in the 'by model' / 'by run' section render in the same color as the table border (theme.sidebarBorder), making them visually invisible.

TDD discipline: write the failing test FIRST, watch it fail, then fix, then watch it pass.

## Diagnosis to run BEFORE editing
- Read src/tui/metrics-modal.ts. The bar-drawing line is around line 764: `putLine(screen, row, innerLeft + 2, barRoom, bar(item.cost, maxCost, Math.min(barWidth, barRoom)), t.sidebarBorder)`. The fg color is `t.sidebarBorder` — same as the surrounding box border. Hence invisible against the border-colored table chrome.
- The `bar(value, max, width)` helper is at line 164. Read it. It returns a string of `█` characters (or similar full-block chars) sized proportionally.
- Theme: read src/tui/theme.ts. Identify what accent/palette colors are available beyond `sidebarBorder`. Likely candidates: `commandPrefix`, `statusRunning`, `statusError`, an accent palette array, or per-status colors. The spec asks for 'a per-row hue indexed by the row's label' — pick from the existing theme palette; do NOT introduce new theme keys.
- Strategy: implement a small pure helper `barColor(label: string, theme: Theme): string` that hashes the label (e.g. djb2: `[...label].reduce((h,c)=>(h*33+c.charCodeAt(0))|0, 5381)`) and indexes into a small fixed palette pulled from existing theme keys. Example palette: `[t.statusRunning, t.commandPrefix, t.statusError, t.sidebarText]` — pick 4 distinct theme keys that are visually distinct from `sidebarBorder`. Two rows with the same label will get the same color (deterministic).
- Replace the `t.sidebarBorder` arg in the `putLine` call with `barColor(item.label, t)` (or whatever the row variable is named in scope).

## Files to modify
- src/tui/metrics-modal.ts — add the `barColor` helper near the top of the file (after imports), use it in the existing bar-rendering call (~line 764), and `export` it for testability.

## Tests (write FIRST, watch fail)
- tests/unit/tui-metrics-bars.test.ts — NEW file. Use bun:test. Approach:
  1. Import `barColor` from src/tui/metrics-modal.ts (added by your fix).
  2. Build a fake theme with distinct values for each palette key (`{ sidebarBorder: '#111', statusRunning: '#222', commandPrefix: '#333', statusError: '#444', sidebarText: '#555', /* …all required Theme fields… */ }`). Type it via `satisfies Theme` or import the `Theme` type from src/tui/theme.ts and construct a complete object. Do NOT use `as any` or `as unknown as`. If filling every Theme field is tedious, narrow the helper's signature to accept only the palette subset it actually reads — e.g. `barColor(label: string, theme: Pick<Theme, 'statusRunning' | 'commandPrefix' | 'statusError' | 'sidebarText'>)` — and test against the narrowed type. The call site in metrics-modal.ts still passes the full theme; structural typing accepts that.
  3. Assertions:
     - `barColor('claude-opus', theme) !== theme.sidebarBorder` — bar is never the border color (note: only meaningful if `sidebarBorder` is in scope of the helper's signature; if the helper doesn't see it, instead assert the result is one of the palette colors and the palette doesn't include sidebarBorder).
     - `barColor('claude-opus', theme) === barColor('claude-opus', theme)` — deterministic.
     - For at least two distinct labels, `barColor(labelA, theme) !== barColor(labelB, theme)` — different rows get different colors. Pick labels that you've confirmed map to different palette buckets via your hash; document the chosen labels and palette in your handoff.
  4. The test must FAIL on `main` (barColor doesn't exist; pre-fix the call site uses `t.sidebarBorder` directly).

Run the test BEFORE editing the implementation to confirm it fails. Record.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/tui-metrics-bars.test.ts` — green after fix.
- `bun test tests/unit/tui` — full TUI test dir green.
- `bun test` — full suite green. No new failures.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` — no NEW errors vs baseline.
- Manual visual: deferred. Note in handoff.

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- Do NOT add new theme keys to src/tui/theme.ts. Use only existing theme color fields.
- Do NOT change the bar character (`█`) or the bar's width logic — just the color.
- Do NOT introduce a gradient (the spec calls it a nice-to-have; ship per-row hue only).
